content.full_text:
December 15, 1977

Honorable James B. Scott  
Associate Justice  
Court of Appeal  
First Appellate District  
455 Golden Gate Avenue  
San Francisco, CA 94102

Dear Justice Scott:

At its December 7, 1977, meeting the Fair Political Practices Commission considered the question of whether gifts of food, travel and lodging made to the spouse of an official in connection with the official's participation in a seminar or panel are reportable on the official's Statement of Economic Interests. The Commission decided that such gifts to the official's spouse are not reportable by the official.

As I believe you are aware, Commission regulations (2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18728) exempt from reporting food, travel and lodging provided to an official in connection with his or her participation in or speaking at an in-state event, seminar or panel. The regulation does not exempt from reporting food, travel and lodging provided to an official's spouse who accompanies him or her to such an event. However, based upon the Cory opinion (Opinion Requested by Ken Cory, 2 PPPC Opinions 48, No. 75-094-A, April 22, 1976) which generally exempts from reporting gifts made to an official's spouse and the policy, embodied in Section 18728, that an official is unlikely to be improperly influenced by food, travel and lodging provided in connection with panels and seminars in which they participate, the Commission decided that the provision of food, travel and lodging to an accompanying spouse should not be reportable.

Because of this Commission decision, it is not necessary for you to amend your 1977 Statement of Economic Interests to report the value of food and accommodations.

The letter states:

"given to your wife in connection with your participation in the California Academy of Appellate Lawyers workshop.

If you have any further questions, please do not hesitate to call me.

Sincerely,

Lee C. Rosenthal
Acting Chief
Legal Division

LCR:plh"

Court of Appeal

September 19, 1977

Mr. Ted Prim, Staff Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95804

Dear Mr. Prim:

Justice Robert K. Fuglia of the Third Appellate District has informed me, by copy of your letter opinion dated August 31, 1977, that judges attending the workshop sponsored by the California Academy of Appellate Lawyers at Ojai in June of 1976 must report the value of food and accommodations given to our wives.

I was unaware of the need for such reporting when I filed my disclosure earlier this year. Will you please send me the necessary forms so that I may amend my statement of economic interests.

Very truly yours,

James B. Scott

sections.conclusion: The Commission decided that such gifts to the official's spouse are not reportable by the official. Because of this Commission decision, it is not necessary for you to amend your 1977 Statement of Economic Interests to report the value of food and accommodations.
sections.question_synthetic: Must an official report the value of food, travel, and lodging provided to their spouse in connection with the official's participation in a seminar or panel event?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concluded that gifts of food, travel, and lodging provided to an official's spouse in connection with the official's participation in a seminar or panel are not reportable on the official's Statement of Economic Interests, based on the Cory opinion and the policy underlying Section 18728.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document includes both the incoming request letter (September 19, 1977) and the FPPC response letter (December 15, 1977). No formal QUESTION or CONCLUSION section headers present, but question and conclusion content are clearly identifiable. The synthetic question and conclusion are derived from the substantive discussion in the response letter.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Must an official report the value of food, travel, and lodging provided to their spouse in connection with the official's participation in a seminar or panel event?

CONCLUSION: The Commission decided that such gifts to the official's spouse are not reportable by the official. Because of this Commission decision, it is not necessary for you to amend your 1977 Statement of Economic Interests to report the value of food and accommodations.
